analogy: Think of "Geography as a Discipline" through the chapter's own opening images: GEOGRAPHY AS A DISCIPLINE This unit deals with • Geography as an integrating discipline; as a science of spatial attributes • Branches of geography; importance of physical geography UNIT I You have studied geography as one of the components of your Social Science course upto... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Geography as a Discipline is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that gEOGRAPHY AS A DISCIPLINE This unit deals with • Geography as an integrating discipline; as a science of spatial attributes • Branches of geography; importance of physical geography UNIT I

The text explains that you have studied geography as one of the components of your Social Science course upto the secondary stage.

The text explains that you are already aware of some of the phenomena of geographical nature in the world and its different parts.

The text explains that now, you will study ‘Geography’ as an independent subject and learn about the physical environment of the earth, human activities and their interactive relationships.

The text explains that therefore, a pertinent question you can ask at this stage is — Why should we study geography?

The text explains that our lives are affected by our surroundings in many ways.

The text explains that we depend on the resources to sustain ourselves in the surrounding areas.

The text explains that primitive societies subsisted on ‘natural means of subsistence’, i.e. edible plants and animals.

The text explains that with the passage of time, we developed technologies and started producing our food using natural resources such as land, soil and water.

The text explains that we adjusted our food habits and clothing according to the prevailing weather conditions.

The text explains that there are variations in the natural resource base, technological development, adaptation with and modification of physical environment, social organisations and cultural development.

The text explains that as a student of geography, you should be curious to know about all the phenomena which vary over space.

The text explains that you learn about the diverse lands and people.

The text explains that you should also be interested in understanding the changes which have taken place over time.

The text explains that geography equips you to appreciate diversity and investigate into the causes responsible for creating such variations over time and space.

The text explains that you will develop skills to understand the globe converted into maps and have a visual sense of the earth’s surface.

The text explains that primitive human societies were directly dependent on their immediate environment.

The text explains that as you already know, the present society has passed the stage of primitive societies, which were directly dependent on their immediate physical environment for sustenance.

The text explains that present societies have modified their natural environment by inventing and using technology and thus, have expanded the horizon of their operation by appropriating and utilising the resources provided by nature.

The text explains that human beings have claimed their contribution using natural resources.

The text explains that it studies population growth, distribution, density, sex ratio, migration and occupational structure etc.

The text explains that physical geography includes the study of lithosphere (landforms, drainage, relief and physiography), atmosphere (its composition, structure, elements and controls of weather and climate; temperature, pressure, winds, precipitation, climatic types, etc.), hydrosphere (oceans, seas, lakes and associated features with water realm) and biosphere ( life forms including human being and macro-organism and their sustaining mechanism, viz. food chain, ecological parameters and ecological balance).

The text explains that soils are formed through the process of pedogenesis and depend upon the parent rocks, climate, biological activity and time.

The text explains that mountains provide pastures, forests, tourist spots and are sources of rivers providing water to lowlands.

The text explains that temperature and precipitation ensure the density of forests and quality of grassland.

The text explains that we study oceans which are the store house of resources.

The text explains that besides fish and other sea-food, oceans are rich in mineral resources.

The text explains that soils are renewable resources, which influence a number of economic activities such as agriculture.

The text explains that the study of physical geography is emerging as a discipline of evaluating and managing natural resources.

The text explains that physical environment provides resources, and human beings utilise these resources and ensure their economic and cultural development.

The text explains that accelerated pace of resource utilisation with the help of modern technology has created ecological imbalance in the world.

The text explains that can you convert time into space and vice versa?

The text explains that (i) You observe every day in your surroundings that there is variation in natural as well as cultural phenomena.

The text explains that all the birds and animals you see, are different.

The text explains that all these different elements are found on the earth.

The text explains that can you now argue that geography is the study of “areal differentiation”?

The text explains that (ii) You have already studied geography, history, civics and economics as parts of social studies.

The text explains that attempt an integration of these disciplines highlighting their interface.

The text explains that (i) Prepare a map of India showing the distribution of different types of forests.

The text explains that (ii) Write about the economic importance of forests for the country.

The text explains that (iii) Prepare a historical account of conservation of forests in India with focus on Chipko movements in Rajasthan and Uttaranchal.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Fundamentals of Physical Geography, Chapter 1: Geography as a Discipline

cases.point: In India, Himalayas have acted as great barriers and provided protection but the passes provided routes to the migrants and invaders from Central Asia. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch1 pg 3)

schemes.name: As
schemes.point: As a social science discipline, geography studies the ‘spatial organisation’ and ‘spatial integration’. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch1 pg 3)

prelims_traps: Do not treat GEOGRAPHY AS A DISCIPLINE and GEOGRAPHY AS A DISCIPLINE 3 as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Geography as a Discipline; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Geography as a Discipline through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use GEOGRAPHY AS A DISCIPLINE and GEOGRAPHY AS A DISCIPLINE 3 to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
